item.title: major arcana
item.description:
The major arcana are the 22 trump cards in a tarot deck, numbered 0 (The Fool) through 21 (The World). They represent archetypal themes, life passages, and psychological forces that operate at a larger scale than the everyday situations depicted in the minor arcana.

The major arcana cards include The Magician, The High Priestess, The Empress, The Emperor, The Hierophant, The Lovers, The Chariot, Strength, The Hermit, Wheel of Fortune, Justice, The Hanged Man, Death, Temperance, The Devil, The Tower, The Star, The Moon, The Sun, Judgement, and The World. Each carries a cluster of meanings that readers interpret in the context of the question asked and the card’s position in the spread.

item.title: minor arcana
item.description:
The minor arcana are the 56 suit cards in a tarot deck, divided into four suits — typically Wands, Cups, Swords, and Pentacles (names vary by deck tradition). Each suit contains numbered cards from Ace through Ten and four court cards (Page, Knight, Queen, King).

The four suits are conventionally associated with broad domains of experience:

  • Wands — action, creativity, will, energy
  • Cups — emotion, relationships, intuition, the inner life
  • Swords — thought, conflict, clarity, communication
  • Pentacles — material reality, work, health, resources

Where the major arcana point to large-scale forces and life themes, the minor arcana address the day-to-day: specific situations, practical challenges, and the texture of lived experience. A reading heavy with minor arcana suggests that the situation is grounded in choices and circumstances the querent can directly act on.

item.title: orientation
item.description:
Orientation refers to whether a tarot card appears upright or reversed (upside-down) when revealed in a spread. In traditions that read reversals, a reversed card modifies the card’s meaning — typically by introducing delay, internalization, blockage, or the shadow side of the card’s upright theme.

Not all readers use reversals. Some traditions read all cards upright, treating the card’s position in the spread and its relationship to neighboring cards as sufficient for nuance. Others treat reversals as essential, arguing that without them the deck can only speak in one register and loses half its expressive range.

item.title: spread
item.description:
A spread is a layout that assigns interpretive roles to card positions in a tarot reading. Each position in the spread carries a meaning — past, present, future; situation, obstacle, advice; self, other, outcome — and the card drawn into that position is read through that meaning.

Spreads range from a single card (drawn for a quick reflection or daily practice) to elaborate layouts of ten or more positions. The three-card spread (commonly read as past/present/future or situation/action/outcome) is the most widely used format for quick readings. The Celtic Cross, a ten-card layout, is the most well-known complex spread in the Western tarot tradition.
